aten::cudnn_convolution (14 register_pre.note: Rank-1 dedup representative accounts for 27.768% (1,143,809 ns, 28 kernels); 13 unique shape groups contribute the remaining 38.8%

operators: aten::cudnn_convolution (all 14 register_pressure nodes, ~66.6% of attributed time)

bottleneck.description: All 14 cudnn_convolution operators dispatch cuDNN's FP32 NHWC implicit-GEMM kernel with 238 registers/thread, hard-capping theoretical occupancy at 24% (A100 allows 64 warps/SM; 238 regs × 32 threads/warp × occupancy limit = 1 warp per SM at full register count). Additionally, every conv forward coerces layout via convertTensor_kernel because the model was not initialized with channels_last memory format — cuDNN receives NCHW tensors and must reorder to NHWC before computation.

evidence.secondary_issue: layout_overhead — convertTensor_kernel launches on every conv forward pass (confirmed by architecture_notes: cuDNN NCHW→NHWC coercion path)

transformation.description: Convert model and input tensor to channels_last (NHWC) memory format. This eliminates convertTensor_kernel launches and signals cuDNN to select its NHWC-native kernel path, which uses fewer registers and higher occupancy than the coercion path.
transformation.code_hint: memory_format is a tensor property that TorchDynamo does not trace; it must be applied before torch.compile() is called. torch.compile() will then see channels_last inputs and propagate the layout through conv layers automatically.

estimated_impact.convertTensor_kernel_launches_eliminated: all (one per conv per forward pass, 14 unique shape groups)
estimated_impact.occupancy_recovery_note: Routing to NHWC-native path reduces registers/thread from 238 to the native path's register budget, allowing cuDNN autotuner to select higher-occupancy algorithms

optimizations.notes: Apply before OPT-2 (BF16) so that the dtype conversion and cuDNN kernel selection both take effect on a model already in the correct memory layout. Channels_last is a no-op for non-4D tensors (e.g., Linear weight matrices) — safe to apply model-wide.

operators: aten::cudnn_convolution (all 14 register_pressure nodes, ~66.6% of attributed time)
aten::addmm (8 tensor_core_idle nodes, ~4.95% of attributed time)

bottleneck.description: Two distinct dtype-related bottlenecks coexist. (1) All conv operators run the FP32 implicit-GEMM path with 238 regs/thread and occupancy capped at ~24%. BF16 routes cuDNN to its sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16 HMMA Tensor Core kernel, which uses materially fewer registers and unlocks higher occupancy. (2) All aten::addmm operators dispatch ampere_sgemm_32x128_tn (FP32 SIMT) with tensor_core_active_pct=0.0 and waves_per_sm=0.009 — these GEMMs are near-zero compute utilization and will immediately benefit from HMMA routing.

evidence.conv_tensor_core_active_pct_range: 57.4% to 72.4% (FP32 Tensor Cores are active, but FP32 TC on A100 is 19.5 TFLOPS vs. 312 TFLOPS for BF16/FP16 TC)

transformation.description: Cast the entire model and input to BF16. For conv layers this switches cuDNN from the FP32 implicit-GEMM path (238 regs/thread, ~24% occupancy) to the HMMA path (sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16, lower register footprint, ~50% occupancy on A100). For addmm/Linear layers this switches from ampere_sgemm_32x128_tn (FP32 SIMT, tensor_core_active_pct=0.0) to the BF16 cuBLAS HMMA path. BF16 is preferred over FP16 for training-forward stability, but either dtype activates HMMA Tensor Cores.
transformation.code_hint: Apply after model.to(memory_format=torch.channels_last) so the channels_last layout is preserved during dtype casting. No FX graph pass required — dtype change on the model propagates to all parameter and buffer tensors. torch.compile() will see BF16 inputs and select BF16-optimized cuDNN/cuBLAS kernels automatically. Note: if the model has tied embeddings, audit before applying — tied weights share storage across dtype cast. This model (conv+BN+linear) has no tied embeddings, so no contraindication.

estimated_impact.conv_occupancy_recovery: ~24% → ~50% (2.1× warp throughput on the register-limited kernel)
estimated_impact.note: Gain on conv is occupancy-limited, not memory-bandwidth-limited (DRAM throughput is only 7–18% for conv). Occupancy recovery from register pressure relief is the primary mechanism, not bandwidth.

optimizations.notes: cuDNN on Ampere (sm_80) routes BF16 inputs to sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16 (WGMMA precursor). The addmm GEMM is tiny (16×256 × 256×10, waves_per_sm=0.009) — even after BF16 routing, absolute gain here is small but latency will improve because HMMA has lower instruction latency than SIMT sgemm on this op size.

bottleneck.description: Batch normalization is the second-largest consumer at 23.9% of attributed time, but Inductor has already fused BN+ReLU into a single Triton kernel (triton_poi_fused__native_batch_norm_legit_no_training_relu_4) and the triage classifies it as well_optimized with 65.9% occupancy and 60.3% DRAM throughput. However, because inference mode is confirmed (operator name is _native_batch_norm_legit_no_training, i.e., training=False), BN parameters can be mathematically folded into the preceding conv weights and biases at model construction time. This eliminates the BN computation entirely — the fused Triton kernel disappears from the kernel launch list.

transformation.description: Fold BatchNorm parameters into the preceding Conv2d weight and bias using the closed-form inference formula. For each conv→BN pair: W_folded = W_conv × (gamma / sqrt(running_var + eps)); b_folded = (b_conv - running_mean) × (gamma / sqrt(running_var + eps)) + beta. After folding, set conv.bias = b_folded, conv.weight = W_folded, and remove the BN module from the model. This is safe because training=False means running_mean and running_var are fixed.
transformation.location: get_model_and_input(), applied to the nn.Module BEFORE torch.compile()
fx_steps: # For each (Conv2d, BatchNorm2d) sequential pair in the model:
def fold_bn_into_conv(conv: nn.Conv2d, bn: nn.BatchNorm2d) -> nn.Conv2d:
bn.eval()  # ensure running stats are in eval mode
# Reshape scale for conv weight multiplication: (C_out, 1, 1, 1)
bias = conv.bias if conv.bias is not None else torch.zeros(conv.out_channels)
# Walk the model and replace conv+BN pairs:
# (Caller must adapt traversal to the specific model's Sequential/named_children layout)
transformation.code_hint: Apply BEFORE torch.compile() and BEFORE OPT-1/OPT-2 dtype/layout casts, OR after — the fold formula is dtype-agnostic. If applying after OPT-2 (BF16), ensure running_mean/running_var are also cast to BF16 before the fold to avoid mixed-dtype arithmetic in the fold itself. Remove the nn.BatchNorm2d modules from the nn.Sequential after folding to prevent double-application. The Triton fused kernel (triton_poi_fused__native_batch_norm_legit_no_training_relu_4) will no longer be generated since the BN op is absent from the FX graph.

estimated_impact.note: Upper bound assumes Inductor cannot re-fuse a replacement elementwise op into a new kernel. Since the existing kernel is BN+ReLU fused, after folding the ReLU remains as a standalone elementwise and Inductor will likely re-fuse it into the subsequent op. Conservative estimate accounts for that re-fusion recovering ~40% of the BN kernel time.

optimizations.notes: OPT-3 is independent of OPT-1 and OPT-2 in correctness but should be applied to the model before dtype/layout casts to keep the fold arithmetic in FP32 for numerical accuracy, then cast the folded model to BF16 in OPT-2. Order: OPT-3 (fold) → OPT-1 (channels_last) → OPT-2 (BF16) → torch.compile().

bottleneck.description: Even after BF16 dtype promotion and channels_last layout, the small addmm GEMMs (16×256 × 256×10, waves_per_sm=0.009) will remain wave-starved because the problem size is too small to saturate A100 SMs regardless of dtype. Additionally, Inductor's default compilation mode may not exhaustively search cuBLAS/cuDNN algorithm libraries for the specific conv shapes present (64×64 and 32×32 spatial, 64/128/256 channels). max-autotune forces Inductor to benchmark all available algorithms and select the fastest.

evidence.note: sm_throughput for conv is already 62% — max-autotune benefit there is algorithm-selection marginal gain. Primary benefit is on small addmm GEMMs and overall Inductor codegen quality.

transformation.description: Compile with torch.compile(mode='max-autotune') to enable exhaustive cuBLAS/cuDNN algorithm search and Triton autotuning. Also enable TF32 for any remaining FP32 paths as a belt-and-suspenders fallback.
transformation.code_hint: max-autotune increases compilation time significantly (minutes vs. seconds for default mode). Use only in production inference scenarios with stable input shapes. allow_tf32 is a no-op once BF16 is applied (OPT-2) but provides coverage if any FP32 ops remain in the graph after compilation.

estimated_impact.note: Expected gain is marginal for well-utilized conv kernels (already at 62% SM throughput) but may be meaningful for Triton-generated BN/ReLU kernels and the small addmm ops. Impact is workload-specific and requires empirical validation.

optimizations.notes: Apply after OPT-1, OPT-2, OPT-3 so that autotuning benchmarks the optimized model (BF16 + channels_last + BN-folded), not the original FP32 NCHW model. Autotuning at FP32 NCHW would select algorithms that are irrelevant after dtype/layout changes.

application_order.rationale: Fold in FP32 for numerical accuracy before any dtype cast
channels_last must be set before torch.compile(); dtype cast in next step preserves the layout
Cast after channels_last so cuDNN sees both NHWC layout and BF16 dtype simultaneously, selecting the optimal HMMA NHWC kernel
Autotune the already-optimized model; allow_tf32 flags set here as fallback

combined_estimated_impact.note: Conservative estimate sums OPT-1 (329,139 ns) + OPT-2 (1,177,766 ns) + OPT-3 (591,303 ns) conservative values. Expected estimate assumes higher BF16 occupancy recovery for conv and full BN elimination with no Inductor re-fusion overhead. Optimizations interact — channels_last (OPT-1) and BF16 (OPT-2) combined change cuDNN kernel selection synergistically; applying both simultaneously may yield more than the sum of parts.

global_notes: All duration values from ncu application-mode replay are 2–5× longer than actual execution due to replay overhead — use for relative comparison and bottleneck identification only, not absolute latency prediction.
A100 SXM4-80GB (Ampere sm_80): 108 SMs, 2 TB/s HBM2e, 312 TFLOPS BF16/FP16 Tensor Core peak, 19.5 TFLOPS FP32 TC. Ridge point ~156 FLOP/byte (BF16 TC).

The existing FP32 Tensor Core activity (57–72% for the larger conv shapes) confirms cuDNN is already using Tensor Core hardware on Ampere even at FP32 — but FP32 TC delivers 19.5 TFLOPS vs. 312 TFLOPS for BF16 TC (16× theoretical speedup; practical speedup after memory bandwidth limits: ~2–4× on these shapes).

warp_cycles_per_instruction is null in this profile — ncu application-mode replay did not collect smsp__average_warp_latency_due_to_* counters. eligible_cycles_pct was used as the latency-bound proxy.

The 6 well_optimized below-threshold conv ops (3×64, 64×3×3 stem convs, ranks 17–22) at ~0.97–0.98% each use convolve_common_engine_float_NHWC with 80 registers/thread and 32–65% occupancy — these are already near hardware limits for their size and are not optimization targets.

Layer deduplication is active in this profile: aten::cudnn_convolution_0 (rank 1, kernel_count=28) is the dedup representative for a group of identical conv layers; its 28 kernels reflect propagated metrics from all duplicates. The 13 remaining conv entries (ranks 3–14, kernel_count=3 each) are unique shape groups.
